macro_events.event_summary: Iran claims the Strait of Hormuz is closed with vessels attempting transit coming under fire, threatening ~20% of global seaborne oil flows.
primary_drivers: Crude oil supply shock / risk premium spike
Tanker rate surge from rerouting and war-risk insurance
Defense demand and geopolitical risk-off flows

beneficiaries.rationale: Large integrated with significant non-Gulf upstream production (Permian, Guyana); leveraged to Brent spike without proportional Hormuz transit risk to its own barrels. Earnings re-rate on sustained >$90 oil.
Similar to XOM — Permian/US domestic-weighted upstream benefits directly from Brent risk premium; limited Iranian/Qatari LNG exposure relative to peers.
Pure-play Permian producer with no Middle East exposure; highest torque to WTI among large-caps, and WTI-Brent spread typically widens on Hormuz events favoring US barrels.
Frontline tanker fleet benefits from surging VLCC/Suezmax day rates as Hormuz closure forces rerouting around Africa and spikes war-risk insurance premiums on remaining transits.
International Seaways crude tanker rates spike on ton-mile demand explosion; direct beneficiary of any prolonged Gulf disruption via spot market exposure.
Cheniere — US LNG export volumes become critical substitute as ~20% of global LNG (Qatar) transits Hormuz; European and Asian buyers bid up US cargo prices.
Defense prime with direct exposure to PAC-3, THAAD, and naval missile systems likely deployed/ordered in any Gulf military escalation; historical pattern of gains on Middle East kinetic events.

losers.rationale: Delta Air Lines — jet fuel is ~25% of operating costs; unhedged exposure to crude spike directly compresses margins. Also most international/long-haul exposed US major.
United has highest international route mix among US carriers including Gulf-adjacent routes; fuel cost shock plus potential airspace closures compound margin hit.
Southwest historically under-hedged on fuel vs peers; domestic low-cost model has least pricing power to pass through fuel spikes to leisure travelers.
Carnival — bunker fuel is major cost input and Middle East/Red Sea itinerary cancellations already pressure cruise operators; consumer discretionary hit from gasoline prices compounds.
Dow Chemical — naphtha/ethane feedstock costs rise with oil spike while demand destruction from global growth scare hits volumes; classic chemicals margin squeeze.
Walmart — diesel-driven logistics costs rise across fleet and supplier network; low-income consumer (core customer) most hurt by gasoline price spike, pressuring discretionary basket.

While EVs benefit from high gas prices long-term, Tesla has meaningful Shanghai production exposure and China imports significant Gulf crude — any Chinese industrial slowdown from oil shock hits deliveries.

headlines: Iran says Strait of Hormuz is closed again as vessels attempting to cross come under fire

macro_events.event_summary: Iran war and $4 gasoline are crushing US consumer discretionary spending, with limited time left to revive the consumer economy before lasting damage sets in.
primary_drivers: Elevated gasoline prices acting as a regressive tax on consumer wallets
Geopolitical risk premium in oil supporting energy producers while pressuring consumer-facing names
Discretionary spending compression shifting share toward staples and value retailers

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with significant upstream leverage to sustained crude price elevation from Iran conflict; every $10/bbl uplift flows materially to earnings.
Pure-play US E&P with near-100% upstream exposure — highest beta to the sustained oil price premium from Middle East disruption among large caps.
Trade-down beneficiary: $4 gas compresses discretionary wallets, driving middle-income consumers into Walmart grocery and essentials, historically a demonstrated pattern in prior gas spikes.
Deep-discount retailer gains traffic as gas-squeezed consumers consolidate trips and seek lowest unit price; direct revenue beneficiary of stressed low-income cohort.
Active Iran war implies sustained/elevated munitions replenishment demand (PAC-3, THAAD, Javelin); direct revenue line exposure to Middle East conflict tempo.
Permian-weighted producer with high operating leverage to crude; Buffett-backed balance sheet means upside flows to equity rather than debt paydown drag.

losers.rationale: Cruise operator with double hit: jet/bunker fuel cost inflation and discretionary travel demand destruction as consumers redirect wallet share to gasoline and essentials.
Jet fuel is ~25-30% of airline opex and Iran-driven crude spike hits directly; simultaneously leisure travel demand softens as consumer discretionary budgets compress.
Low-income consumer cohort (core QSR customer) is most gas-price sensitive; recent quarters already showed traffic declines in this segment, now amplified.
Premium-priced fast casual most vulnerable when consumers trade down to grocery or cheaper QSR; ticket-size compression risk to comps.
High-ticket discretionary furniture retailer — exactly the category consumers defer when gas + geopolitical anxiety dominates the wallet.
Online home goods highly discretionary; weak consumer backdrop plus any shipping fuel surcharge pressure squeezes already thin margins.
OTA revenue tied to leisure travel bookings which fall when gas prices dominate household budgets and war headlines deter international travel.
Premium athleisure sits squarely in the discretionary wallet share losing to gas and essentials; aspirational buyer is the marginal shopper here.

headlines: Iran war, $4 gas, took all the fun out of the U.S. consumer economy. There's only so much time to bring it back

macro_events.event_summary: Congress passed a short-term extension of FISA Section 702, the foreign surveillance authority, keeping the program alive without major reform.
primary_drivers: Continuation of government surveillance contracts/data flows
Regulatory status quo for defense and intelligence contractors
No meaningful privacy-law tightening on tech platforms

beneficiaries.rationale: Palantir's Gotham platform is directly used by US intelligence and defense agencies for signals/data analysis workflows that rely on Section 702-collected data. Continuation of the authority preserves a key data pipeline underpinning recurring government contract revenue.

Booz Allen derives the majority of revenue from US federal intelligence/defense contracts, including IC systems integration tied to FISA-authorized collection programs. Extension preserves contract continuity.
Leidos runs large classified IT and analytics contracts for NSA and other IC customers where Section 702 data is processed; extension avoids disruption to these programs.
CACI has substantial SIGINT and intelligence analyst contract exposure; reauthorization keeps the underlying collection pipeline intact.

headlines: Three things to know about FISA Section 702: Congress passes short-term extension of controversial surveillance program

macro_events.event_summary: Tariff refunds are set to be distributed to US importers, with some companies receiving them ahead of others.
primary_drivers: One-time cash refund to importers that previously paid tariffs (working capital boost)
Reduced COGS recognition / potential margin recovery on prior quarters
Signal that tariff regime is being unwound or narrowed

beneficiaries.rationale: Five Below sources a high share of discretionary goods from China; as a heavy direct importer, it would receive material refunds relative to its market cap and margin base.
Dollar Tree/Family Dollar is among the most China-import-exposed discount retailers; tariff refunds would directly reverse margin compression flagged in prior guidance.
Best Buy imports consumer electronics largely from China/Asia and has been explicit about tariff drag; refunds flow straight to gross margin and cash.
RH disclosed substantial tariff costs on furniture imports; a refund is a direct earnings and cash-flow tailwind given its leveraged balance sheet.
Williams-Sonoma imports a large share of home furnishings from Asia and previously absorbed/passed through tariffs; refunds boost FY cash position.
Hasbro's toys are predominantly imported from China; refunds reverse a well-publicized tariff drag and improve near-term FCF.
Auto-parts retailers pay tariffs on Chinese-sourced aftermarket components; AAP's thin margins amplify the percentage impact of any refund recapture.

headlines: Tariff refunds are coming: Here’s who will get them first

macro_events.event_summary: Trump expects Fed chair nominee Kevin Warsh to deliver rate cuts, with speculation on how Warsh might engineer them.
primary_drivers: Lower expected policy rates → lower discount rate on long-duration assets
Weaker USD from dovish Fed expectations
Concerns about Fed independence → steeper curve / inflation expectations rising

beneficiaries.rationale: Leveraged play on crypto rally from Fed independence concerns and liquidity expectations; transaction revenue scales with crypto volumes.

losers.rationale: Large banks with significant deposit franchises see NIM compression from rate cuts; JPM specifically guided that NII is rate-sensitive on the way down. Less acute than regionals but material.
Schwab's earnings are highly levered to short rates via cash sweep economics; aggressive cuts directly compress net interest revenue.

headlines: Trump expects his Fed chair nominee to cut interest rates. Here’s how Kevin Warsh might try to do it.
